questions.text: Why is the raw periodogram called an inconsistent estimator of the true PSD?
choices: Its variance at a fixed frequency does not shrink as the recording length grows
It cannot be computed for signals longer than a few thousand samples
It only works for signals with zero mean
questions.explanation: Consistency means variance shrinks to zero as sample size grows. The raw periodogram's variance at a fixed frequency stays roughly the same size as the PSD it's estimating no matter how long the recording is — recording longer only sharpens resolution, it doesn't steady the estimate.

questions.text: Truncating a signal to a finite recording window, before any noise is even considered, causes:
choices: Spectral leakage — energy at one frequency smears into neighbouring frequencies
No distortion at all, since truncation only removes samples you didn't have anyway
A shift in the true frequency of every spectral component
questions.explanation: Truncation is multiplication by a rectangular window in time, which is convolution by that window's spectrum in frequency — this smears a clean spectral line into a lobe, purely from the recording having a start and an end.

questions.text: Choosing a smoother taper (e.g. Hann) instead of a rectangular window mainly trades:
choices: Lower spectral leakage for a wider main lobe (coarser resolution)
Lower computational cost for slightly more leakage
Nothing — smoother windows dominate rectangular windows with no tradeoff
questions.explanation: Every window shape trades main-lobe width (resolution) against side-lobe height (leakage). A Hann window's main lobe is roughly twice as wide as a rectangular window's, in exchange for much lower leakage into distant frequencies.

questions.text: Bartlett's method reduces periodogram variance by:
choices: Averaging the periodograms of several non-overlapping segments of the recording
Applying a single very long FFT to the entire recording at once
Discarding every other sample before computing one periodogram
questions.explanation: Splitting the recording into K segments and averaging their periodograms turns one inconsistent estimate into K roughly independent estimates of the same PSD — averaging independent estimates is what actually reduces variance.

choices: Overlapping the segments (typically 50%) and tapering each one before its periodogram
Using segments that grow longer over the course of the recording
Replacing averaging with taking the maximum periodogram value at each frequency
questions.explanation: Welch's method overlaps segments to recover samples that a taper would otherwise down-weight at each segment's edges, on top of Bartlett's segment-averaging idea.

questions.text: You split a recording into K = 16 non-overlapping segments and average their periodograms. Roughly by what factor does the variance shrink compared to one periodogram over a single segment?
questions.explanation: Averaging K roughly independent estimates divides variance by about K, so 16 segments give roughly a 16x variance reduction — at the cost of 16x coarser frequency resolution per segment than the full recording.

questions.text: A 32-second recording is split into 8 equal, non-overlapping segments for Bartlett's method. How many seconds long is each segment?
questions.explanation: 32 seconds split into 8 equal segments gives 4 seconds each — the frequency resolution of each segment's periodogram is set by this shorter length, not the full 32 seconds.

questions.text: One word: splitting a long recording into more, shorter segments to average their periodograms improves variance but costs you frequency ___.
questions.explanation: Shorter segments mean coarser frequency resolution — the bias-variance tradeoff at the heart of Bartlett/Welch averaging.

questions.text: Cumulative review: the power spectral density these estimators are all trying to approximate is defined (via Wiener–Khinchin) as:
choices: The Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function of a wide-sense stationary process
The Fourier transform of the process's mean value
The variance of the process at a single instant in time
questions.explanation: From the Wiener–Khinchin theorem (wiener-khinchin-and-psd module): for a WSS process, the PSD is the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function R_x(τ). Every estimator in this module — periodogram, Bartlett, Welch — is an attempt to approximate that same quantity from finite, noisy data.
